qa_pairs.question: How do users request a full A100-40gb GPU, or multiple A100-40gb GPUs, for a computing task on the Narval cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: To request a single A100-40gb GPU, users should employ the Slurm option `--gpus=a100:1`. For multiple A100-40gb GPUs per node, options include `--gpus-per-node=a100:2`, `--gpus-per-node=a100:3`, or `--gpus-per-node=a100:4`. To request multiple A100-40gb GPUs that can be spread across the cluster, use `--gpus=a100:n`, replacing 'n' with the desired total number.

qa_pairs.question: What is Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) technology on Narval, what are the available instance sizes, and how can a user request a single 2g.10gb MIG instance?
qa_pairs.answer: Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) technology on Narval configures GPU nodes to allow a single physical GPU to be partitioned into smaller, isolated instances. The available instance sizes are 1g.5gb (offering 1/8 of the compute power with 5 GB of GPU memory), 2g.10gb (2/8 of the compute power with 10 GB of GPU memory), and 3g.20gb (3/8 of the compute power with 20 GB of GPU memory). To request a single 2g.10gb MIG instance for a computing task, use the Slurm option `--gpus=a100_2g.10gb:1`.

qa_pairs.question: How can users monitor their computing tasks on the Narval cluster, and why is it important to adjust resource requests based on actual usage?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can monitor their CPU and GPU computing tasks in real-time or review past tasks through the Narval portal, available at https://portail.narval.calculquebec.ca/. This portal allows for visualizing CPU core usage, memory utilization, and GPU usage. It is crucial to monitor and adjust resource requests to maximize resource utilization, minimize wait times in the queue, and ensure efficient use of allocated resources. For example, if a user requests four CPU cores but only uses one, they should adjust their submission file accordingly.
